dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 4 responding for a patient unresponsive in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Derek, an EMT with the rescue squad. What led to the 911 call today?
My cousin was out grocery shopping this morning and I returned to find him sitting unresponsive on the couch.
He's unresponsive to normal conversation and only makes non-purposeful movements when stimulated.
The left eyelid does not open on its own. We can lift it manually but see no spontaneous movement.
No voice response to any level of verbal interaction. He's not making any sounds.
There was one clear purposeful movement when he turned his head toward his name being called.
The airway is patent and the patient is continuing to breathe without assistance.
Respirations are regular, with a rate of 18-20, and without labored breathing.
Chest movement is equal bilaterally and without signs of distress so far.
Systolic is over 300, per both automated and manual readings. The readings exceed the device's maximum.
Skin is warm, pink, and dry. Radial pulses are strong, bounding, and regular.
Pulse is 96 now, strong and regular.
SpO2 is 96% and this is while breathing ambient air.
Blood glucose is at 134 currently.
ECG is showing a normal sinus rhythm and no arrhythmia found.
I was gone for shopping at 7:30 and returned at 3 p.m., when I found him unresponsive.
The pupils are equal and reactive to light at about 4 mm each.
GCS is at 8. Eyes are 2, verbal is 1, motor is 5.
There is no muscle tone in the left eyelid, but the right eyelid has some tone.
Skin color is warm and pink, no signs of cyanosis or visible discoloration.
We are working to establish IV access in his right bicep. This will help deliver fluids and medications if needed.
The heart rate has now increased to 102, but continues in regular rhythm.
We established IV access in his right bicep using a 20-gauge IV, and it is flushed and patent.
Based on the field assessment, this is a possible stroke. Following protocol 3-5, heading to VCU with lights and sirens.
IV line is in place and working. We're giving fluids at KVO.
The EKG is still showing a normal sinus rhythm with no changes detected.
Directing to VCU using lights and sirens. Patient positioned to maximize comfort.
Stair chair is secured and patient is on the cot with restraints applied.
ETA is approximately 4 minutes with all vital signs stable for now.
Patient has bowel incontinence noted. This has been documented.
Blood glucose readings were not obtainable. Equipment returned no result.
Patient is currently incontinent of bowel and not reporting pain.
Pupils are 4 mm, equal, and reactive but need to be manually opened.
No pain reported despite brain involvement suspicion.
IV site is intact and flowing at KVO. No observed issues.
Breathing remains 18-20 per minute with current SpO2 of 94%.
Heart rate is 98. EKG is still showing sinus rhythm.
SpO2 is now 94%. This was already noted in the last check.
I've called in the stroke alert using our cell phone.
Will transfer patient to Trauma Bay 1 using a sheet pull method with five caregivers.
Patient is currently lying on their back. All restraints have been released.
Heart rate is at 98, SpO2 at 94%, and glucose remains unidentifiable.
All vital sign data is being sent now and is en route to the hospital system.
The hospital has personnel ready in Trauma Bay 1.
All medications and treatments are given and confirmed. Any questions about care provided?
Patient is now completely handed over to the ED team.
This was a critical stroke alert. Thanks for the collaboration.
Despite no vocalization, the field impression is consistent with a possible hemorrhagic stroke.
Patient is unresponsive at baseline with only minimal purposeful movements to loud stimuli.
Airway remains patent with no signs of obstruction or secretions noted.
Temperature is normal with no signs of active infection or fever.
The patient is on several medications, including atorvastatin, but there are no contraindications reported.
No medications have been administered in the field per protocol limits.
Second blood glucose attempt still returned no reading. Further attempts deemed unnecessary.
No pain reported as the patient is non-responsive.
No signs of abdominal distress or rigidity observed.
Both pupils showed equal and reactive responses to direct light.
Normal bilaterality with no anisocoria.
Unable to complete neck exam due to lack of cooperation and responsiveness.
The patient last ate that morning around 7:30 and has not had anything since.
Patient secured onto the ambulance cot by two RFD crew and we ensured proper stability during climb.
4-lead ECG was applied and used for continuous monitoring throughout.
Repeat BP measures both automated and manual still reading over 300 with no signs of drop.
No new changes in respiratory effort noted.
Continuous EKG remains stable with regular and normal sinus patterns.
Patient is unresponsive but shows brief responsiveness to loud stimuli.
This fits the pattern of a possible stroke per our standing protocol and field impression.
Airway is patent, self-maintained within normal limits.
Chest movement is bilaterally equal and without abnormal sounds or flare.
Head and neck are clear with no masses or significant deformities detected.
Color is normal with pink appearance and no jaundice or pallor seen.
Stroke suspicion field impression remains due to non-verbal and non-purposeful limb use.
Eyes require manual opening. Pupils show equal and reactive response.
Patient makes no vocalizations. Swallowing function unknown.
The patient is not engaging any motor tasks and remains unresponsive overall.
P is the highest level achieved in AVPU scores observed in field.
Blood pressure remains unmeasurable at our equipment's limit.
History is suspicious for transient ischemic attack, supporting field impression.
IV has remained stable throughout transit and is at KVO.
EKG has remained stable with no evolution in pattern or signs of infarction.
Strong, regular pulse is unchanged from initial time.
Heart rate remains high, currently at 98-102 in range.
SpO2 fluctuates slightly but remains overall in safe range (94-96%).
Left eyelid shows no muscle tone. Right has some consistency.
IV access remains intact with no changes noted since transport initiation.
Transport is uninterrupted and direct to VCU. Patient was loaded smoothly.
A full medical report is being transmitted and stroke alert is active.
Patient is expected to arrive in three to four minutes predicting optimal care.
Incontinence was noted; limited eye opening and motor tone confirmed.
Patient is in a safe, supported position for transport.
Receiving facility is alerted and prepared for a stroke alert patient.
All alarm systems are active in the field for a timely stroke alert.
The patient presents with possible hemorrhagic stroke per field suspicion.
Patient had one purposeful movement after stimulus, indicating some neurological function.
IV site remains intact and functioning.
Left eyelid has no tone while right eyelid has mild tone.
Handing over patient with stroke suspicion and stable vital signs.
Patient will receive optimal care, all parameters checked and reported.
Per protocol, administrative and medical steps are complete.
